role_hints.role: Building and home inspectors (owners and staff)
role_hints.oews_basis: 47-4011 Construction and Building Inspectors 2.05% of the 4-digit 5413 wage bill — but 5413 is engineering-dominated; 47-4011 is this code's signature occupation and within 541350 proper inspectors are plausibly ~65-75% of wages (hypothesis; 6,523 of 7,623 firms are under-5-employee owner-operator shops)
role_hints.note: The physical walk-through — attic, crawlspace, roof, systems testing — cannot be done by AI and is the product's legal/contractual core. But report writing, photo annotation, defect narration and code citation are a large share of the hours and are near-fully automatable; the within-role fraction should come from the field-time vs. report-time split, plausibly 0.2-0.35.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.72% at 4-digit; owner comp largely SDE outside wages; hypothesis ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Realtor-relationship marketing and inspector recruiting persist; scheduling and dispatch automate.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-xxxx office/admin occupations in the 5413 tail; hypothesis ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Booking, confirmations, report delivery and payment collection are already platform tasks (ISN-style inspection software).

role_hints.role: Specialty technical staff (commercial PCA, engineers on staff)
role_hints.oews_basis: 17-2051 Civil Engineers 12.8% at 4-digit is parent contamination, but a small real engineering presence exists in commercial property-condition-assessment firms; hypothesis ~5%
role_hints.note: Commercial PCA report production (ASTM E2018) is document-heavy and AI-exposed; field verification is not.

source_hints.source: InterNACHI (International Association of Certified Home Inspectors) member resources and surveys
source_hints.why: The largest inspector association — member counts, fee polls and technology/AI-report-writing tool uptake.

source_hints.source: ASHI (American Society of Home Inspectors) member data
source_hints.why: Second major association; demographics and practice surveys.

source_hints.source: Porch Group / ISN (Inspection Support Network) published inspection-volume and pricing data
source_hints.why: The dominant inspection-workflow platform's telemetry — per-inspection fee benchmarks and volume trends; Porch acquired ISN, verify what it still publishes.

source_hints.source: NAR (National Association of Realtors) Profile of Home Buyers and Sellers / inspection-waiver statistics
source_hints.why: Demand-side evidence: share of purchases with inspections and the waiver trend in competitive markets — the main volume risk, unrelated to AI.
Only visible owner-age evidence for a solo-heavy trade; expect thin data and say so.

source_hints.source: Franchise disclosure documents (Pillar To Post, WIN Home Inspection, AmeriSpec, HouseMaster/Neighborly)
source_hints.why: Franchise systems are the visible consolidation pattern — unit economics, royalty loads and resale values; Neighborly's HouseMaster acquisition shows strategic interest.
Small inspection-business comps; expect SDE-based listings.

capture_questions: Pricing is a flat per-inspection fee (a few hundred dollars) paid by the homebuyer in a one-shot transaction: when AI cuts report-writing time roughly in half, does the operator keep that as margin (fee is set by local going rates, not hours) — or does inspector oversupply and realtor fee-shopping pass it through as lower fees? Note the fee was never hours-priced, which argues for margin retention.

Who owns demand — the buyer hires once, but the realtor referral relationship drives most flow (subject to state RESPA-adjacent rules on referral steering): is the durable asset the firm's realtor network, and does it survive an acquisition and the founding inspector's exit?

What stops buyers from relying on AI photo/video analysis plus seller disclosures instead of a paid inspection — is the moat the physical access and testing, the liability-backed written opinion (E&O), state licensing requirements, or lender/insurer requirements, and which of those actually binds in the dominant residential segment?
Is inspection volume itself the bigger risk — waived inspections in competitive markets and falling existing-home sales — such that AI economics are second-order to transaction-volume exposure?
terminal_value_question: A physical human walk-through cannot be done by AI, and most states license home inspectors — but the paid product is an opinion attached to a real-estate transaction, and demand is set by whether buyers (and their agents and insurers) still commission it. The deciding mechanisms: state licensing plus contract contingency norms hold the human requirement in place, while inspection waivers, iBuyer/institutional purchasing and AI-assisted seller disclosure could shrink the commissioned base. Does mature AI cut the inspector's report-production cost (margin gain on an intact product), or erode the share of transactions that buy a human inspection at all? Answer with the waiver-rate and transaction-volume trend lines, not capability speculation.
special_notes: Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 (engineering-dominated; 47-4011 only 2.05% at parent level) and misstates this code — inspectors dominate the real wage bill; rebuild and flag as hypothesis.

Demand is derivative of existing-home transaction volume — the 2021-2022 boom then 2023-2025 slump moved inspection counts massively for reasons unrelated to AI; normalize any revenue evidence against NAR existing-home sales, and treat inspection-waiver rates as a separate structural headwind.

EBITDA normalization: overwhelmingly SDE owner-operator businesses (6,523 of 7,623 firms under 5 employees); n_band is just 40 firms — the acquirable pool is multi-inspector regional firms and franchise operators, and single-inspector books have minimal transferable value since the realtor relationships are personal.

Sub-segment mix: residential home inspection (dominant, licensed in most states, consumer-paid) vs. commercial property condition assessment (ASTM E2018, lender-driven, engineering-adjacent) vs. specialty niches (radon, sewer scope, energy audits — often upsells). Exposure and pricing differ; score the dominant residential segment and note the mix.

Boundary confusion: municipal/code-enforcement inspectors are government employees (not in this code); engineering firms doing structural inspections are 541330; pest/termite inspection is 561710; home energy raters may sit in 541690 — association data (InterNACHI) spans licensed individuals, not firms, so firm-level economics need care.
Watch for state licensing variation: a handful of states still have no home-inspector license — the human-requirement moat is contract-and-custom in those markets, not statute.
